content.body:
Central nervous system (CNS):
• Brain (cerebrum, cerebellum, medulla)
• Spinal cord

Peripheral nervous system (PNS):
• Sensory (afferent) neurons: receptor → CNS
• Motor (efferent) neurons: CNS → effector

Reflex arc: receptor → sensory neuron → relay neuron (spinal cord) → motor neuron → effector
Reflex is FAST because it bypasses brain

content.title: Neuron Structure
content.body:
Cell body (soma): nucleus, organelles
Dendrites: receive signals from other neurons
Axon: transmits impulse away from cell body (can be 1m long)
Myelin sheath: fatty insulation, speeds transmission (saltatory conduction)
Nodes of Ranvier: gaps in myelin
Synaptic knobs: release neurotransmitters

Refractory period: after firing, Na⁺/K⁺ pumps restore resting potential

content.title: Brain Regions
content.body:
Cerebrum (cerebral cortex): thinking, memory, language, voluntary movement
Cerebellum: coordination, balance
Medulla oblongata: automatic functions (heartbeat, breathing, swallowing)
Hypothalamus: temperature regulation, hunger, thirst
Broca's area: speech production

questions.question: The CNS consists of
questions.answer: brain and spinal cord
questions.explanation: CNS: brain (cranium) and spinal cord (vertebral canal). PNS: all other nerves

questions.question: Reflex arcs bypass the brain to be
options: faster — allows rapid response without conscious thought
questions.answer: faster — allows rapid response without conscious thought
questions.explanation: Reflex: sensory→relay neuron (spinal cord)→motor. No brain processing needed → response in ~15ms vs ~150ms for voluntary

questions.question: Myelin sheath speeds nerve impulse because
options: it insulates axon, forcing impulse to jump between Nodes of Ranvier (saltatory conduction)
questions.answer: it insulates axon, forcing impulse to jump between Nodes of Ranvier (saltatory conduction)
questions.explanation: Saltatory: Latin for jumping. Impulse leaps from node to node instead of travelling continuously → much faster

questions.question: Sensory neuron carries impulses
questions.answer: from receptor/sense organ to CNS
questions.explanation: Afferent (sensory) neurons: carry information FROM the environment/body TO the CNS

questions.question: Cerebellum's main function is
questions.answer: balance and coordination of movement
questions.explanation: Cerebellum: coordinates fine motor control, balance, posture, smooth muscle movements. Damaged → ataxia

questions.question: Medulla oblongata controls
options: heartbeat, breathing and other autonomic functions
questions.answer: heartbeat, breathing and other autonomic functions
questions.explanation: Medulla: vital automatic functions — if damaged, death. Controls heart rate, breathing rate, blood pressure

questions.question: In a reflex arc, order is
questions.answer: receptor→sensory→relay→motor→effector
questions.explanation: Reflex arc sequence: receptor → sensory neuron → relay neuron (spinal cord) → motor neuron → effector

questions.question: Dendrites receive signals; axons
options: transmit impulse away from cell body to next neuron or effector
questions.answer: transmit impulse away from cell body to next neuron or effector
questions.explanation: Dendrites: INPUT. Axon: OUTPUT. Axon terminals release neurotransmitters into synapse

questions.question: Multiple sclerosis is caused by
options: immune system attacking myelin sheath, slowing nerve conduction
questions.answer: immune system attacking myelin sheath, slowing nerve conduction
questions.explanation: MS: autoimmune demyelination. Plaques of damaged myelin slow/block nerve impulse transmission

questions.question: Refractory period after nerve impulse means
options: neuron cannot fire again immediately (Na⁺/K⁺ balance being restored)
questions.answer: neuron cannot fire again immediately (Na⁺/K⁺ balance being restored)
questions.explanation: After action potential: Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump restores resting potential. During this period, neuron cannot respond to new stimuli

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body:
Synapse: gap between neurons
Neuromuscular junction: neuron-muscle synapse

Synaptic transmission:
1. Action potential reaches synaptic knob
2. Ca²⁺ influx triggers vesicle fusion
3. Neurotransmitter (ACh) released into synaptic cleft
4. NT binds postsynaptic receptors
5. New action potential generated (if threshold reached)
6. NT broken down (AChE) or reuptake

Excitatory vs inhibitory neurotransmitters

content.title: Neurotransmitters
content.body:
Acetylcholine (ACh): neuromuscular junction, parasympathetic
Noradrenaline: sympathetic 'fight or flight'
Dopamine: reward, movement (deficient in Parkinson's)
Serotonin: mood, sleep (low in depression)
GABA: inhibitory (calm, anti-anxiety)

Drugs:
Nicotine: mimics ACh
SSRIs (Prozac): prevent serotonin reuptake
Atrylcholine inhibitors: nerve agents (organophosphates)

content.title: Sense Organs
content.body:
Eye: photoreceptors (rods=dim/B&W, cones=colour)
Ear: hair cells in cochlea detect sound vibration
Nose: olfactory receptors detect airborne chemicals
Tongue: taste buds (sweet, sour, salty, bitter, umami)
Skin: mechanoreceptors (touch, pressure), thermoreceptors, nociceptors (pain)

Accommodation (eye): ciliary muscles change lens shape for near/far focus

questions.question: Synaptic transmission direction is
options: unidirectional (presynaptic → postsynaptic) because NT released from one side only
questions.answer: unidirectional (presynaptic → postsynaptic) because NT released from one side only
questions.explanation: Synapses are unidirectional: vesicles only on presynaptic membrane → NT always flows presynaptic to postsynaptic

questions.question: Calcium ions (Ca²⁺) at synapse trigger
questions.answer: vesicle fusion and neurotransmitter release
questions.explanation: Ca²⁺ enters synaptic knob when action potential arrives, triggering exocytosis of NT-containing vesicles

questions.question: Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) at synapse
questions.answer: breaks down ACh (ending signal)
questions.explanation: AChE hydrolyses ACh → choline + acetate. Essential to end signal; organophosphate nerve agents inhibit AChE → continuous stimulation

questions.question: Parkinson's disease involves deficiency of
questions.answer: dopamine (in substantia nigra, basal ganglia)
questions.explanation: Parkinson's: progressive loss of dopamine neurons → tremor, rigidity, slow movement. Treated with L-DOPA (dopamine precursor)

questions.question: Rods vs cones in retina: rods are for
options: detecting dim light (low intensity) in black and white
questions.answer: detecting dim light (low intensity) in black and white
questions.explanation: Rods: very sensitive, ~120 million, no colour. Cones: 3 types (RGB), ~6 million, concentrated in fovea, colour vision in bright light

questions.question: SSRIs treat depression by
options: preventing reuptake of serotonin, increasing its concentration in synapse
questions.answer: preventing reuptake of serotonin, increasing its concentration in synapse
questions.explanation: SSRIs (Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors): block transporter that returns serotonin to presynaptic neuron → more serotonin available

questions.question: Threshold potential in neurons means
options: action potential only fires if stimulus reaches minimum threshold voltage (~-55mV)
questions.answer: action potential only fires if stimulus reaches minimum threshold voltage (~-55mV)
questions.explanation: All-or-nothing principle: below threshold = no action potential; at/above threshold = full action potential regardless of stimulus size

questions.question: Accommodation of the eye for near vision requires
options: contracting ciliary muscles → lens thickens (more curved) → greater refraction
questions.answer: contracting ciliary muscles → lens thickens (more curved) → greater refraction
questions.explanation: Near vision: ciliary muscles contract, suspensory ligaments slacken, lens rounds up → more refractive power → focuses near objects

questions.question: Which sensory organ detects body position and movement (proprioception)?
questions.answer: Muscle spindles and joint receptors
questions.explanation: Proprioceptors: muscle spindles (detect stretch), Golgi tendon organs, joint receptors → sense of body position without looking

questions.question: Fight or flight response is mediated by
questions.answer: noradrenaline/adrenaline (sympathetic system)
questions.explanation: Sympathetic: noradrenaline → heart rate↑, pupils dilate, bronchi dilate, digestion↓, ready for action. Opposite of parasympathetic

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria

questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Nervous System. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Nervous System concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Nervous System builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 3.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Nervous System. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 3.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Nervous System. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Nervous System include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Nervous System. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Nervous System. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Nervous System concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Nervous System builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 6.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Nervous System. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 6.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Nervous System. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Nervous System include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Nervous System. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.
